Visit this sheltered sandy bay within the park known for calm waters, mangrove-lined shores, and excellent snorkeling for beginners.
Observe large schools of jacks, trevally, and barracuda sweeping through the deeper channels and canyon walls during seasonal migrations.

food.text: Grilled Red Sea Fish with Lemon
food.detail: Freshly caught local fish such as grouper or snapper grilled whole and served with lemon, olive oil, and aromatic herbs at park-adjacent restaurants in Sharm El-Sheikh.
This popular Egyptian street food combines rice, lentils, pasta, and spiced tomato sauce—a filling vegetarian option available at nearby town cafes.
An assortment of seasoned shrimp, squid, and fish with tahini-based dips, pita bread, and fresh salads reflecting Bedouin and Mediterranean influences.
Strong mint tea served in small glasses alongside fresh dates—a traditional refreshment offered by Bedouin guides and park staff throughout the day.
Slow-cooked fava beans seasoned with garlic and cumin, served with bread for breakfast—a staple protein source for early-morning dive and snorkel trips.

culture.detail: The park is a strictly protected reserve; do not touch corals, remove shells, or stray from designated snorkeling and diving zones, and heed all ranger instructions.
Use only authorized park guides and boat operators who know safe routes, marine life behavior, and can interpret Bedouin and Egyptian maritime traditions respectfully.
While swimwear is appropriate in the water, wear lightweight cover-ups and closed shoes when exploring cliff areas and interacting with staff, reflecting Egyptian coastal customs.
Red Sea diving has deep roots in Sinai's Bedouin heritage; ask guides about traditional fishing practices and how modern conservation protects ancestral marine knowledge.

places.name: Tiran and Sanafir Islands
places.description: Twin islands in the Red Sea offering exceptional snorkeling and diving with vibrant coral reefs and abundant fish populations, accessible by boat from Sharm El-Sheikh. The islands provide pristine underwater experiences and are popular day-trip destinations.

activities.text: Coral Reef Snorkeling at Tiran Island
activities.detail: Explore vibrant shallow coral gardens teeming with tropical fish, sea turtles, and rays in crystal-clear waters ideal for beginners and experienced snorkelers.
Experience dramatic underwater drop-offs and strong currents between Tiran and Sanafir where pelagic fish, sharks, and large groupers congregate.
Dive the wreck of a patrol vessel in the shallow waters between the islands, now encrusted with corals and inhabited by reef fish.
Relax on pristine sandy beaches with minimal crowds, enjoy picnicking, and observe Bedouin heritage sites on this less-developed island.
Discover nocturnal marine life and bioluminescent organisms illuminating the reefs after sunset during evening boat excursions.
Photograph and identify endemic Red Sea fish species including parrotfish, angelfish, butterflyfish, and endemic goby populations.

food.detail: Fresh-caught grouper or snapper seasoned with garlic, lemon, and herbs, grilled over charcoal and served with tahini sauce on day-trip boats.
Egyptian comfort food combining rice, lentils, pasta, and spiced tomato sauce, commonly served as a hearty lunch during island excursions.
Assorted dips including hummus, baba ganoush, and muhammara accompanied by pita bread and fresh vegetables, standard fare on diving tours.
Fresh Red Sea shrimp fried in olive oil with feta cheese and tomatoes, a local specialty reflecting the islands' seafaring culture.
Traditional sweet mint tea served with fresh dates, offered as refreshment by local guides and boatmen between diving activities.

culture.detail: The islands are protected Egyptian territory with strict marine reserve regulations; follow all guide instructions and avoid touching corals or collecting shells.
Use established dive shops and boat operators from Sharm El-Sheikh who hold proper permits, as independent access to the islands is restricted for environmental protection.
Greet boat crew and local staff with 'As-salamu alaikum' (peace be upon you) and 'Shukran' (thank you), showing respect for Bedouin hospitality traditions.
Wear a rash guard or light cover-up when not in the water, as these are sacred Egyptian waters frequented by observant Muslim locals.

places.name: Nuweiba
places.description: A coastal town serving as a gateway to Bedouin culture and the gateway for ferry connections to Jordan and Saudi Arabia across the Red Sea. The town offers beautiful beaches, relaxed vibes, and excellent access to desert adventures and snorkeling.

activities.detail: Explore vibrant coral gardens and tropical fish in the shallow waters just off Nuweiba's main beach, accessible directly from shore or by short boat trip.
Take the overnight or day ferry across the Red Sea to the Jordanian port town, a convenient gateway for exploring Petra and Wadi Rum.
Hike through a spectacular desert canyon featuring striped rock formations in shades of red, orange, and yellow located about 30 km inland from Nuweiba.
Spend an evening with local Bedouin families to learn traditional crafts, share meals, and experience desert hospitality in a authentic setting.
Trek to a hidden freshwater spring surrounded by date palms and granite cliffs, offering swimming and cooling off after desert exploration.
Stroll along the sandy waterfront at sunset, enjoying relaxed coastal atmosphere with views across the Red Sea toward Saudi Arabia.
Drive into the Eastern Desert to spot Bedouin settlements, dramatic mountain formations, and remote wadis inaccessible by regular vehicles.

food.detail: Lamb slow-cooked underground with vegetables and spices, a traditional Bedouin dish served during special occasions and often prepared in camps.
Locally caught grouper, snapper, or emperor fish grilled whole over charcoal and seasoned simply with lemon, garlic, and olive oil.
Egyptian street food combining rice, lentils, pasta, and chickpeas topped with spicy tomato sauce and crispy fried onions, widely available at local eateries.
Strong Arabic coffee flavored with cardamom served alongside fresh or dried dates, the traditional welcome drink in Bedouin camps and homes.
Slow-cooked fava beans seasoned with garlic and lemon, typically eaten for breakfast and served with pita bread and fresh vegetables.

culture.detail: Always accept hospitality graciously, remove shoes when entering tents, and avoid pointing at people; Bedouins place high value on courtesy and generosity.
While beaches are casual, cover shoulders and knees when visiting Bedouin camps or walking through town out of respect for local Islamic traditions.
Greetings like 'Assalamu alaikum' (peace be upon you) and 'Shukran' (thank you) are deeply appreciated and open doors to genuine local interactions.
In markets and with Bedouin guides, negotiating prices is a normal part of commerce and often an enjoyable social exchange rather than a conflict.

places.name: Blue Lagoon
places.description: A stunning sheltered lagoon near Dahab featuring brilliant turquoise waters and excellent snorkeling opportunities with minimal currents, ideal for swimmers of all levels. The shallow, pristine waters and abundant marine life make it a memorable Red Sea experience.

activities.detail: Explore the shallow turquoise waters teeming with colorful reef fish, corals, and occasional sea turtles in one of the Red Sea's most accessible snorkeling spots.
Wade into the calm, crystal-clear waters that are ideal for non-swimmers and children due to minimal currents and gradual depth increase.
Relax on the sandy shore surrounded by sheltering cliffs and enjoy the Mediterranean-style beach atmosphere with stunning turquoise backdrop.
Watch the sun descend over the Red Sea from the surrounding limestone cliffs for dramatic photography and romantic evening atmosphere.
Observe marine life from a boat without getting wet by viewing the seafloor and coral gardens through transparent hull panels.
Capture vibrant images of tropical fish, anemones, and coral formations in shallow, well-lit waters ideal for both amateur and professional underwater cameras.

food.detail: Fresh whole fish caught locally and chargrilled with lemon, garlic, and olive oil, typically served with rice and salad at beachfront restaurants.
A hearty Egyptian street food combining rice, lentils, pasta, and chickpeas topped with tomato sauce and crispy fried onions, popular quick meal in Dahab.
Plump Mediterranean shrimp sautéed with tomatoes, feta cheese, and herbs, a signature seafood dish reflecting both Egyptian and Greek influences.
Slow-cooked fava beans seasoned with garlic, cumin, and olive oil, traditionally eaten for breakfast and widely available at local cafés near the lagoon.
Assorted small plates including hummus, baba ganoush, tabouleh, and freshly baked pita bread, perfect for sharing while overlooking the turquoise waters.

culture.detail: While tourists wear standard beachwear, be mindful that modest swimwear is appreciated; cover-ups like sarongs are respectful when leaving the water in local areas.
Simple phrases like 'Marhaba' (hello) and 'Shukran' (thank you) are warmly received by local vendors and boatmen who appreciate visitors' cultural respect.
Many local businesses close briefly during the five daily Islamic prayer times; plan restaurant visits and activity bookings around these schedules, particularly midday and sunset.
Haggling is customary and expected at beach vendors and local shops, but it should be done with good humor and respect as part of genuine cultural exchange.

places.name: Taba
places.description: A small border town at the northernmost tip of Sinai, strategically located between Israel and Egypt with access to unique desert landscapes and beach activities. The town serves as a cultural and geographical crossroads with relatively quiet charm and good diving opportunities.

activities.text: Diving and Snorkeling at Taba Heights Reef
activities.detail: Explore vibrant coral gardens and tropical fish in the clear Red Sea waters just offshore, suitable for both beginners and experienced divers.
Tour the Mamluk fortress ruins and remains of a 12th-century stronghold located on an island accessible by boat from Taba harbor.
Drive through the dramatic granite mountains and wadis surrounding Taba, including visits to Wadi Zaranik and colorful canyon formations.
Experience the unique geopolitical position by walking or driving across the only land border between Israel and Egypt at this strategic checkpoint.
Relax on the town's main sandy beach with clear turquoise waters and views of the surrounding desert landscape and Israeli coastline.
Wade into shallow coral gardens directly from the beach for close encounters with Red Sea marine life without requiring boat access.

food.detail: Fresh local grouper caught from the Red Sea, grilled whole with lemon, garlic, and olive oil, served with Egyptian bread and tahini.
Assorted appetizers featuring marinated octopus, grilled shrimp, hummus, baba ganoush, and fresh Egyptian salad typical of Sinai coastal cuisine.
Slow-cooked lamb with dried apricots, almonds, and warming spices, reflecting the desert heritage of the region's nomadic communities.
The Egyptian comfort food of mixed rice, lentils, pasta, and spiced tomato sauce topped with fried onions found at modest local eateries.
Whatever fish was caught that morning, prepared simply grilled or in a light tomato-based sauce at waterfront restaurants.

culture.detail: Taba is a heavily monitored military zone; avoid photographing checkpoints, military installations, or the border fence, and carry your passport at all times.
While English is spoken in tourist areas, locals appreciate visitors who use phrases like 'As-salamu alaikum' (peace be upon you) and 'Shukran' (thank you).
Although Taba is more liberal than inland Egypt, women should cover shoulders and knees respectfully, especially when away from the beach or tourist zones.
The region's identity is deeply tied to Bedouin traditions; visiting desert settlements and engaging respectfully with local guides supports authentic cultural exchange.

restaurants.name: Shark Restaurant
restaurants.description: Beachfront seafood institution offering grilled fish and fresh catches with stunning Red Sea views and sunset dining.
restaurants.mustOrder: Grilled Sea Bass with garlic butter

restaurants.name: Abou Ashraf
restaurants.description: Authentic family-run eatery in the old souk serving traditional Egyptian fare with locals and budget travelers.

restaurants.name: Peppermill Steakhouse
restaurants.address: Montaza Lofts, Naama Bay, Sharm El-Sheikh
restaurants.description: Upscale steakhouse with prime cuts, extensive wine list, and refined ambiance catering to luxury resort clientele.
restaurants.mustOrder: Prime rib with truffle mashed potatoes

restaurants.name: Fares Restaurant
restaurants.description: Casual backpacker favorite near the lagoon serving simple Egyptian dishes and fresh juices at rock-bottom prices.

restaurants.name: Nesima Resort Restaurant
restaurants.description: Resort dining with lagoon views offering Mediterranean and Middle Eastern cuisine in relaxed beachfront setting.

restaurants.name: Al-Fanous Restaurant
restaurants.description: Local favorite serving hearty Egyptian staples and fresh seafood with friendly service and no tourist markup.

restaurants.name: Basata Beach Restaurant
restaurants.description: Eco-friendly beachfront restaurant using organic ingredients and offering a peaceful alternative to mainstream tourist venues.

restaurants.name: Camel Restaurant
restaurants.description: Authentic casual grill house famous for charcoal-grilled meats and traditional sides with packed local atmosphere.

restaurants.name: The Reef Restaurant
restaurants.description: Waterfront dining specializing in fresh daily catches prepared simply to highlight quality with excellent service standards.

restaurants.name: Bedouin Restaurant
restaurants.description: Casual spot serving traditional Bedouin dishes and mezze in rustic setting with authentic regional cooking.

restaurants.name: Saïd El Souhgy
restaurants.description: Lagoon-side institution known for fresh fish, friendly atmosphere, and reasonable prices with spectacular sunset views.
restaurants.mustOrder: Grilled squid with garlic and cilantro

restaurants.name: Cilantro Restaurant
restaurants.description: Contemporary Asian restaurant offering Thai, Chinese, and Japanese dishes with creative presentations in sophisticated setting.

restaurants.name: El Masrien Restaurant
restaurants.description: Simple family restaurant serving authentic Egyptian comfort food popular with local workers and budget travelers.

restaurants.name: Marakesh Restaurant
restaurants.description: Upscale Moroccan dining with tagines, couscous, and traditional ambiance featuring belly dancing and evening entertainment.
restaurants.mustOrder: Lamb tagine with apricots and almonds

regions.name: Cairo & Giza
regions.bestTimeToVisit: October to April offers pleasant temperatures for exploring pyramids and museums.

regions.name: Alexandria
regions.bestTimeToVisit: November to March provides ideal coastal weather and fewer crowds.

regions.name: Nile Delta
regions.bestTimeToVisit: October to April is best for visiting the agricultural heartland and avoiding summer heat.

regions.name: Suez Canal & Sinai
regions.bestTimeToVisit: October to March offers comfortable temperatures for exploring Port Said and Ismailia.

regions.name: Red Sea Riviera
regions.bestTimeToVisit: October to April provides perfect conditions for diving and snorkeling.

regions.name: Upper Egypt
regions.bestTimeToVisit: October to April is ideal for temple visits and Nile cruises with moderate temperatures.

regions.name: Luxor & Thebes
regions.bestTimeToVisit: October to April offers comfortable weather for exploring pharaonic temples and tombs.

regions.name: Aswan & Nubia
regions.bestTimeToVisit: October to March provides relief from extreme heat while exploring Nubian temples and Lake Nasser.

regions.name: Western Desert
regions.bestTimeToVisit: October to April is best for desert exploration and visiting oases.

regions.name: Sinai Peninsula
regions.bestTimeToVisit: October to April offers pleasant temperatures for hiking Mount Sinai and exploring Bedouin culture.

top10.name: Giza Plateau
top10.description: Home to the Great Pyramids of Khufu, Khafre, and Menkaure, plus the Sphinx, representing the pinnacle of ancient Egyptian engineering and artistry. It is the most iconic archaeological site in Egypt and attracts millions of visitors annually seeking to witness these 4,500-year-old wonders.

top10.name: Cairo
top10.description: The sprawling capital of Egypt and Africa's largest city, straddling the Nile River with over 20 million inhabitants and blending ancient pharaonic heritage, Islamic medieval districts, colonial architecture, and modern neighborhoods. Cairo is the cultural and historical heart of Egypt, offering world-class museums, historic mosques, vibrant markets, and an intoxicating urban atmosphere.

top10.name: Luxor
top10.description: The modern city of Luxor sits on the east bank of the Nile and serves as the primary hub for visiting ancient Thebes. It offers excellent hotels, restaurants, and access to the region's most spectacular monuments.

top10.name: Valley of the Kings

image.credit: Museum of New Zealand Te Papa Tongarewa

top10.description: This vast necropolis on the west bank contains 62 royal tombs carved into rock cliffs, including the famous tomb of Tutankhamun. It remains the most iconic archaeological site in Egypt and a must-see for understanding pharaonic burial practices.

top10.name: Aswan
top10.description: Egypt's southernmost major city, Aswan is known for its relaxed atmosphere, stunning Nile views, and proximity to Abu Simbel and the Aswan Dam. The city serves as a gateway to Nubia and offers felucca sailing, colorful markets, and beautiful desert landscapes.

top10.name: Abu Simbel
top10.description: Two colossal rock temples carved directly into a mountainside, dating to Ramesses II's reign, featuring four 67-meter-high statues of the pharaoh at the entrance. This UNESCO World Heritage site is one of Egypt's most iconic monuments and was dramatically relocated in the 1960s to save it from Lake Nasser's flooding.

top10.name: Hurghada
top10.description: Egypt's largest Red Sea resort city and gateway to the Riviera, offering world-class diving, snorkeling, and beach resorts. The Marina and downtown areas buzz with restaurants, shops, and watersports operators catering to international visitors.

top10.name: Sharm El-Sheikh
top10.description: A world-class Red Sea resort city known for its pristine beaches, coral reefs, and luxury accommodations. It's the premier destination for diving, snorkeling, and water sports in the region with excellent infrastructure and nightlife.

top10.name: Karnak Temple
top10.description: This massive temple complex dedicated to Amun-Ra is the largest religious structure ever built, featuring towering pylons, colossal statues, and the hypostyle hall with 134 columns. Walking through Karnak is essential to comprehending the scale and ambition of ancient Egyptian architecture.

top10.name: Alexandria
top10.description: Egypt's second-largest city and ancient Mediterranean port founded by Alexander the Great, featuring the iconic Citadel of Qaitbay built on the site of the ancient Lighthouse of Alexandria. The city blends Greco-Roman history with Hellenistic culture, offering museums, beaches, and a cosmopolitan atmosphere unmatched elsewhere in Egypt.

valley_of_kings.credit: Museum of New Zealand Te Papa Tongarewa

guide/festivals.json.title: Festivals & events
guide/festivals.json.subtitle: 40 celebrations through the year — pick a day to see what's on.

festivals.name: New Year's Day
festivals.description: National public holiday celebrating the start of the new year with festivities across the country. Banks, government offices, and many businesses close.

festivals.name: Coptic Christmas
festivals.description: The Coptic Orthodox Church celebrates Christmas following the Julian calendar, with special church services and family gatherings. A public holiday in Egypt.

festivals.name: Hanging Church Festival
festivals.description: Celebrations at Cairo's iconic Hanging Church (Al-Muallaqa) in Coptic Cairo with special liturgies and gatherings of pilgrims. Dates vary according to Coptic calendar.

festivals.name: Revolution Day (January 25)
festivals.description: Commemorates the 2011 Egyptian Revolution with rallies, ceremonies, and street celebrations across the country. A major national public holiday.

festivals.name: Abu Simbel Sun Festival
festivals.description: Twice yearly phenomenon where the sun illuminates the inner sanctum of Abu Simbel temple, drawing thousands of visitors to witness this astronomical marvel. Occurs again in October.

festivals.name: Luxor Balloon Festival
festivals.description: Hot air balloon rides over the Valley of the Kings and Luxor's monuments, typically held in cooler winter months with multiple balloons launching at sunrise. Dates vary by operator.

festivals.name: Ismailia International Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates Ismailia's heritage with cultural performances, sporting events, and exhibitions highlighting the Suez Canal's importance. Usually held in winter.

festivals.name: Coptic Easter (Moveable)
festivals.description: The Coptic Orthodox Easter celebration with solemn Holy Week services and joyful resurrection celebrations following the Julian calendar. Date varies yearly.

festivals.name: Sinai Liberation Day
festivals.description: Commemorates Egypt's complete withdrawal from Sinai in 1982 with military parades and nationalist celebrations. A public holiday.

festivals.name: Sham El Nessim
festivals.description: Ancient Egyptian spring festival celebrated the day after Coptic Easter with outdoor picnics, traditional foods, and family gatherings in parks. A public holiday.

festivals.name: Labour Day
festivals.description: International labour day celebrated in Egypt with worker demonstrations and public holidays for rest and celebration.

festivals.name: Eid al-Fitr (Moveable)
festivals.description: Three-day Islamic festival marking the end of Ramadan with family gatherings, new clothes, gifts, and special foods. Major public holiday with festive atmosphere throughout the country.

festivals.name: Eid al-Adha (Moveable)
festivals.description: Four-day Islamic festival of sacrifice commemorating Abraham's willingness to sacrifice his son, celebrated with animal slaughter, feasting, and family visits. Major public holiday.

festivals.name: Islamic New Year (Moveable)
festivals.description: Marks the beginning of the Islamic lunar calendar year with religious observances and some public celebrations. A public holiday.

festivals.name: Prophet Muhammad's Birthday - Mawlid al-Nabi (Moveable)
festivals.description: Celebrates the birth of Prophet Muhammad with religious gatherings, processions, and special sweets distributed throughout Muslim communities. A public holiday.

festivals.name: Cairo International Film Festival
festivals.description: One of Africa's oldest and most prestigious film festivals featuring international and Arab cinema with screenings, premieres, and industry events. Usually held in November.

festivals.name: Revolution Day (June 30)
festivals.description: Commemorates the June 30, 2013 protests and demonstrations with national celebrations and official ceremonies. A public holiday.

festivals.name: Nile Flood Festival (Historical)
festivals.description: Commemorates the ancient Egyptian celebration of the Nile's inundation season with cultural events and historical reenactments. Modern celebrations revive this ancient tradition.

festivals.name: Alexandria International Summer Festival
festivals.description: Multi-week festival featuring music, theatre, dance, and cultural performances celebrating Mediterranean heritage along Alexandria's waterfront. Usually runs through August.

festivals.name: Aswan Nubia Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates Nubian culture with traditional music, dance, crafts, and cuisine in Aswan. Features performances from local Nubian communities.

festivals.name: Hurghada Red Sea Festival
festivals.description: Beach festival celebrating Red Sea tourism with water sports, music, diving competitions, and beach activities. Celebrates marine life and coastal heritage.

festivals.name: Abu Simbel Sun Festival (Second)
festivals.description: Second annual occurrence of the sun phenomenon at Abu Simbel temple when sunlight penetrates the inner sanctum, attracting thousands of visitors.

festivals.name: Sharm El-Sheikh International Triathlon
festivals.description: International sporting event featuring swimming, cycling, and running competitions attracting athletes from around the world. Showcases Sinai's modern tourism infrastructure.

festivals.name: El Gouna Festival of Arts
festivals.description: Contemporary arts festival in the planned resort town featuring visual arts, sculpture, and cultural performances. Celebrates modern Egyptian creativity.

festivals.name: Suez Canal Day
festivals.description: Celebrates the nationalization of the Suez Canal in 1956 with ceremonies, parades, and national pride events in canal cities. A public holiday.

festivals.name: Dendera Temple Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates the Dendera Temple complex with cultural performances and historical reenactments. Honors the Temple of Hathor in ancient Dendera.

festivals.name: Edfu Horus Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates the Temple of Horus with historical reenactments and cultural events highlighting ancient Egyptian religious practices. Performed in the well-preserved temple.

festivals.name: Luxor Luxor Marathon
festivals.description: International marathon run through ancient Thebes with routes passing temples and archaeological sites. Combines sport with sightseeing.

festivals.name: Aswan International Women's Film Festival
festivals.description: Annual film festival showcasing films by and about women with screenings and discussions. Important cultural event for independent cinema.

festivals.name: Wadi El Rayan Eco-Tourism Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates the protected desert ecosystem with eco-friendly activities, bird watching, and nature education. Held in the stunning Wadi El Rayan protected area.

festivals.name: Dakhla Oasis Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates oasis culture with traditional music, crafts, and desert heritage featuring local Bedouin traditions. Showcases unique desert community life.

festivals.name: Nile Delta Agricultural Fair
festivals.description: Celebrates the Nile Delta's rich agricultural heritage with crop exhibitions, farming competitions, and traditional products. Important event for rural communities.

festivals.name: Tanta Sugar Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates Egypt's sugar production with displays, traditional sweets, and family entertainment in Tanta. Features local sugar-based delicacies.

festivals.name: Port Said International Theatre Festival
festivals.description: Prestigious theatre festival featuring Egyptian and international theatrical performances. Celebrates dramatic arts and cultural exchange.

festivals.name: Revolution Day (July 23)
festivals.description: Commemorates the 1952 Free Officers' Revolution that overthrew the monarchy with military parades and nationalist celebrations. A major public holiday.

festivals.name: Armed Forces Day
festivals.description: Commemorates Egypt's armed forces with military parades, ceremonies, and celebrations honoring soldiers and national defense. A public holiday.

festivals.name: Nubian Festival of Culture and Arts
festivals.description: Comprehensive celebration of Nubian heritage with music, dance, traditional crafts, and cuisine featuring the vibrant Nubian community. Highlights unique cultural practices.

festivals.name: Alexandria Mediterranean Tourism Forum
festivals.description: International tourism forum and cultural festival celebrating Alexandria's Mediterranean heritage with exhibitions and performance events. Attracts global visitors.

festivals.name: Egyptian National Day
festivals.description: National holiday celebrating Egyptian sovereignty and national identity with official ceremonies and street celebrations throughout the country.

festivals.name: New Year Eve Celebrations
festivals.description: Grand celebrations ringing in the new year with parties, fireworks, and festivities throughout major cities including Cairo, Alexandria, and resort towns.

guide/history.json.title: A Short History of Egypt
guide/history.json.subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 15 eras that shaped it.

items.label: Predynastic Egypt
items.summary: Hunter-gatherers and early farmers establish settlements along the Nile and develop the foundations of Egyptian civilization.
items.text: As the Sahara dried into desert, human populations concentrated along the Nile Valley, learning to cultivate wheat and barley in the rich floodplain soil. Separate kingdoms emerged in Upper Egypt (the south) and Lower Egypt (the north), each developing distinct pottery styles, religious practices, and social hierarchies. Settlements grew into towns, and evidence of trade networks stretches from Nubia to the Levant. By 3100 BC, these scattered communities were ready to be unified under a single ruler.

items.label: Early Dynastic Period
items.summary: Egypt unifies under the pharaohs, establishing divine kingship and the administrative systems that would endure for millennia.
items.text: King Narmer (or Menes) conquered Lower Egypt and merged it with Upper Egypt, creating history's first nation-state and launching Dynasty I. The pharaoh was understood as a living god, a belief that would structure Egyptian society and religion for three thousand years. The early dynastic pharaohs built mastaba tombs and established Memphis as their capital, creating a centralized bureaucracy to manage irrigation, taxation, and religious life. Writing systems evolved during this period, allowing the government to record laws, inventories, and religious texts with unprecedented detail.

items.label: Old Kingdom
items.summary: Egypt's pyramid age: pharaohs command vast resources to build monumental tombs and establish themselves as divine intermediaries.
items.text: The Old Kingdom saw the construction of Egypt's most iconic monuments, beginning with the Step Pyramid of Djoser and culminating in the Great Pyramids of Giza. These colossal structures required unprecedented organizational capacity, mobilizing tens of thousands of workers, quarrymen, and administrators in carefully orchestrated projects lasting decades. The pharaoh's power reached its zenith, and the state controlled trade, religion, and the irrigation system that kept Egypt prosperous. By the end of the period, however, the cost of maintaining these monuments and a growing priesthood had weakened central authority, leading to the collapse of Dynasty VI.

items.label: First Intermediate Period
items.summary: Central authority fractures as regional governors assert independence, fragmenting Egypt into competing kingdoms.
items.text: The decline of the Old Kingdom triggered roughly 150 years of political chaos, with pharaohs struggling to command loyalty and regional nomarchs (governors) operating as independent rulers. Famine, possibly linked to changes in the Nile's flood pattern, further weakened the state's grip. Art and literature from this era reveal social anxiety and questioning of traditional beliefs, yet it was also a time of cultural innovation as power dispersed to the provinces. Eventually, the kings of Thebes in Upper Egypt grew strong enough to challenge the northern kingdoms.

items.label: Middle Kingdom
items.summary: A cultural renaissance and reunification under Theban dynasties produce Egypt's classical age of art, literature, and stability.
items.text: The Middle Kingdom is often called Egypt's classical period, a time when the pharaohs, particularly those of Dynasty XII, restored central authority and embarked on ambitious building projects, irrigation works, and military expeditions. Egyptian art and literature flourished, with scribes composing The Tale of Sinuhe and other masterpieces that would be studied for centuries. The state developed a more sophisticated bureaucracy and expanded trade networks across the Mediterranean and into Nubia. This era of prosperity and cultural achievement lasted roughly four centuries before external pressure and internal decline destabilized the kingdom.

items.label: Second Intermediate Period
items.summary: Foreign invaders, the Hyksos, occupy northern Egypt while the south remains under Egyptian control, fragmenting the kingdom again.
items.text: The Hyksos, a confederation of Asiatic peoples, gradually infiltrated and then dominated northern Egypt, establishing their own dynasties and ruling from Avaris in the Delta. Rather than a sudden invasion, it was a gradual process of settlement and political consolidation that the Egyptians found deeply humiliating. Southern rulers at Thebes resented foreign occupation but were initially too weak to challenge it. This period of division lasted over a century, but it also introduced Egypt to new technologies like the horse-drawn chariot and composite bow, which would prove crucial in their eventual reconquest.

items.label: New Kingdom
items.summary: Egypt reaches its height of power and territorial expansion under military pharaohs who conquer an empire stretching to Syria and Nubia.
items.text: The New Kingdom began when Ahmose I expelled the Hyksos and reunified Egypt, launching an era of unprecedented imperial expansion and cultural achievement that would last five centuries. Pharaohs like Thutmose III and Amenhotep III commanded armies that conquered territories from Nubia to the Levant, enriching Egypt with tribute and slaves. The period produced some of history's most magnificent monuments, including the Temple of Karnak, the Valley of the Kings, and the mortuary temple of Hatshepsut, one of Egypt's few female pharaohs. This was also the age of figures like Akhenaten, who briefly challenged traditional polytheism, and Tutankhamun, whose intact tomb would astonish archaeologists millennia later.

items.label: Third Intermediate Period
items.summary: Imperial Egypt fragments as foreign invasions, economic decline, and the rising power of the priesthood erode pharaonic authority.
items.text: The end of the New Kingdom saw Egypt losing its Asian territories to the Hittites and later the Sea Peoples, while the priesthood of Amun at Thebes accumulated such wealth and land that they rivaled the pharaoh's power. Subsequent dynasties ruled from the north while Theban priests effectively controlled the south, creating an unusual form of divided government. Libya's influence grew as Libyan mercenaries and settlers became prominent in Egyptian society, eventually founding their own dynasties. Economic troubles and the failure to maintain the irrigation system further weakened central authority, leaving Egypt vulnerable to conquest.

items.label: Late Period
items.summary: Egypt staggers through recurring foreign domination by Assyrians, Persians, and others, though periodic native revivals inspire cultural pride.
items.text: The Assyrian conquest of 664 BC marked the beginning of an era in which Egypt would rarely control its own destiny, yet periods of native rule under the 26th Dynasty saw a remarkable cultural renaissance, with artists and scribes consciously imitating and reviving Old Kingdom styles. The Persian conquest in 525 BC was followed by a brief restoration of native rule, then Persian reconquest, creating a pattern of instability. Despite political turmoil, Egypt remained economically important due to the Nile and control of Mediterranean trade routes, making it a prize repeatedly fought over. By the 4th century BC, Egypt had endured two centuries of foreign rule and was ripe for conquest by a young Macedonian king.

items.label: Ptolemaic Egypt
items.summary: Alexander the Great and his Greek successors rule Egypt, merging Greek and Egyptian cultures into a cosmopolitan Hellenistic civilization.
items.text: Alexander the Great conquered Egypt in 332 BC, and though he remained only briefly, his general Ptolemy founded a dynasty that would rule for three centuries. The Ptolemies, of Macedonian Greek origin, adopted pharaonic titles and customs while establishing Alexandria as the Mediterranean's greatest center of learning and commerce. This was an age of cultural synthesis where Greek philosophy, science, and art mingled with Egyptian religion and traditions, producing remarkable achievements in mathematics, medicine, and literature. The dynasty's final ruler was the famous Cleopatra VII, whose political alliances with Rome and romantic liaisons could not prevent the empire's inevitable conquest by Augustus Caesar.

items.label: Roman and Byzantine Egypt
items.summary: Egypt becomes a province of Rome and later the Byzantine Empire, its wealth supporting the Mediterranean's greatest power.
items.text: Augustus's conquest in 30 BC made Egypt a personal possession of the Roman emperor, transformed from an independent kingdom into a province whose grain fed Rome's population. The Nile's fertility made Egypt phenomenally valuable, and Roman administrators carefully managed irrigation and taxation to maximize revenue. Christianity spread rapidly through Egypt during the later Roman period, and by the Byzantine era, monasticism flourished in the deserts, with Egyptian monks becoming revered figures in the Christian world. When Arab armies arrived in 641 AD, Byzantine Egypt's Christian population, exhausted by religious disputes and heavy taxation, offered surprisingly little resistance to change.

items.label: Islamic Egypt
items.summary: Arab conquest and Islamization transform Egypt's culture, language, and religion, though various dynasties compete for control.
items.text: The Arab conquest introduced Islam and the Arabic language, which gradually became dominant over the next few centuries, reshaping Egyptian identity. The Umayyad and Abbasid caliphates ruled Egypt distantly until the Fatimid dynasty (969–1171 AD) established Cairo as a glittering capital rivaling Baghdad and created a sophisticated state infrastructure. Later, Saladin's Ayyubid dynasty fought the Crusaders and defended the faith, while the Mamluks, originally slave-soldiers, seized power and ruled as a military oligarchy for 250 years, making Egypt wealthy through trade but also vulnerable to Ottoman conquest. Throughout these centuries, Egypt remained culturally and economically vital, a crossroads of Mediterranean, African, and Asian trade.

items.label: Ottoman Egypt
items.summary: The Ottoman Empire rules Egypt as a provincial backwater for three and a half centuries, until Napoleon's invasion sparks modernization.
items.text: The Ottoman conquest reduced Egypt to a provincial afterthought within a vast empire, though Mamluk elites retained significant local power and the Nile's agricultural wealth continued to flow to Constantinople. The region stagnated economically and intellectually for nearly three centuries until Napoleon's invasion of 1798 disrupted Ottoman authority and sparked European interest in Egyptian antiquities, archaeology, and modernization. Muhammad Ali, an Ottoman officer of Albanian origin, seized power in 1805 and transformed Egypt into a quasi-independent state, building a modern army, expanding irrigation, and establishing Muhammad Ali's dynasty as the country's rulers. His successors maintained nominal Ottoman suzerainty while pursuing ambitious development, including the construction of the Suez Canal, which made Egypt strategically crucial to European powers.

items.label: British Occupation and Independence
items.summary: Britain occupies Egypt nominally to protect the Suez Canal, rules for seven decades, and faces growing nationalist resistance demanding independence.
items.text: After Egypt defaulted on its Ottoman debts, Britain intervened militarily in 1882, ostensibly temporarily, but remained for seven decades, controlling the military, irrigation system, and economy while maintaining the facade of Ottoman sovereignty (and later nominal independence after 1922). The occupation sparked Egyptian nationalism, with intellectuals, students, and workers organizing against colonial rule, though the British remained confident in their strategic necessity after the Suez Canal's opening. World War I and II further disrupted Egyptian society, as the British used the country as a military base and conscripted resources for imperial wars. By 1952, nationalist sentiment had reached a breaking point, and a group of army officers led by Gamal Abdel Nasser seized power, promising genuine independence and sweeping social reform.

items.label: Modern Egypt
items.summary: Egypt embarks on an ambitious nation-building project under Nasser, faces wars with Israel, and navigates Cold War politics and modernization.
items.text: Nasser's revolution transformed Egypt from a colonial appendage into an independent state with socialist ambitions, nationalizing the Suez Canal in 1956 and defying Western pressure in the subsequent crisis. He pursued Arab nationalism, regional leadership, and rapid industrialization, though his military defeats by Israel in 1967 damaged his prestige and Egypt's economy. His successor, Anwar Sadat, pragmatically pursued peace with Israel (the 1979 Camp David Accords), reoriented toward the West, and opened Egypt's economy, policies continued by Hosni Mubarak's long regime. Today, Egypt remains the Arab world's most populous nation and a crucial geopolitical player, facing challenges of population growth, water scarcity, religious tensions, and economic inequality, while maintaining its ancient legacy as one of humanity's greatest civilizations.

guide/food.json.title: Food & Drink
guide/food.json.subtitle: How to eat well in Egypt — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Turkish Coffee [Ahwa Turki]
items.text: Ground coffee brewed in a small copper pot with cardamom, served in tiny cups without milk. It's thick, strong, and traditionally prepared right in front of you—watch for the foam rising twice before pouring.

items.label: Café as Social Hub
items.text: Ahwas (coffee houses) are gathering places for locals to drink coffee, smoke shisha, and play backgammon or dominoes. Expect to spend hours there; it's not a grab-and-go culture.

items.label: Fishawi Café, Khan el-Khalili
items.text: Cairo's oldest operating café (since 1773) in the heart of the bazaar, packed with tourists and locals alike. The atmosphere is electric but service is slow—arrive early or expect waits.

items.label: Egyptian Nescafé
items.text: While not traditional, instant coffee is ubiquitous and often served with condensed milk and hot water. Ask for it 'sada' (black) or 'mazbout' (medium sweet) to specify your preference.

items.label: Koshari
items.text: Egypt's national comfort food: a carb-loaded mix of rice, lentils, pasta, and chickpeas topped with spiced tomato sauce and crispy fried onions. It's cheap, filling, and available everywhere—order from busy stalls for the freshest batches.

items.label: Ful Medames
items.text: Slow-cooked fava beans mashed with garlic, lemon, and olive oil, traditionally eaten for breakfast. It's rich, protein-packed, and best scooped up with warm pita bread from a street vendor.

items.label: Ta'ameya [Egyptian Falafel]
items.text: Made from fava beans (not chickpeas like Lebanese falafel), these are lighter, fluffier, and more herby. Stuff them into pita with tahini sauce, pickles, and tomatoes for a complete meal.

items.label: Hygiene & Street Food
items.text: Eat where locals eat—high turnover means fresher food. Avoid anything that's been sitting around, and skip salads from unknown vendors; stick to hot, freshly cooked items.

items.label: Liver Sandwich [Sandwich Kbda]
items.text: Grilled lamb liver chopped fine and served on pita with parsley, onions, and tahini sauce. It's a beloved breakfast item that catches many tourists off-guard but rewards the adventurous.

items.label: Molokhia
items.text: A leafy green vegetable (jute leaves) cooked down into a thick, silky stew with garlic and served over rice or bread. It's Egypt's soul food, traditionally prepared at family gatherings and celebrations.

items.label: Mahshi [Stuffed Vegetables]
items.text: Tomatoes, peppers, and zucchini hollowed out and stuffed with rice, herbs, and sometimes meat, then simmered until tender. Each Egyptian family claims their version is the best.

items.label: Shakshuka
items.text: Eggs poached in a spicy tomato sauce with peppers and onions, often topped with cheese. It's hearty, warming, and excellent for breakfast or lunch.

items.label: Hamam Mahshi [Stuffed Pigeon]
items.text: Whole roasted pigeon filled with bulgur wheat and herbs—a celebration dish with delicate, rich meat. Order it at upscale restaurants; street versions are less reliable.

items.label: Basboosa [Coconut Semolina Cake]
items.text: A sweet, moist cake made from semolina and shredded coconut, soaked in sugar syrup and topped with an almond. It's sticky, indulgent, and the go-to sweet snack at bakeries.

items.label: Konafa [Shredded Pastry Dessert]
items.text: Fine shredded pastry layered with nuts, honey, and sometimes cream, creating a crispy-then-gooey texture. It's messy to eat but absolutely worth the experience.

items.label: Use Your Right Hand
items.text: In Egyptian culture, the left hand is considered unclean and reserved for personal hygiene. Always eat, greet, and gesture with your right hand, even if you're left-handed.

items.label: Sharing & Communal Eating
items.text: Meals are often shared from large platters placed in the center of the table; everyone eats from the same dishes using bread as a utensil. It's considered friendly and builds connection.

items.label: Refusing Food
items.text: Politely declining food can be seen as insulting; always try a small portion if offered. If you're truly full, place your hand over your heart and say 'Shukran' (thank you) with genuine warmth.

items.label: Tea After Meals
items.text: Mint tea (shai bi nana) is traditionally served after dinner to aid digestion; accepting it is polite and shows you've enjoyed the meal. Declining might suggest the food didn't sit well.

items.label: Bargaining in Markets
items.text: At street food stalls or casual eateries, prices are often negotiable, especially if you're ordering multiple items. A friendly smile and willingness to haggle is expected and part of the experience.

items.label: Hibiscus Tea [Karkade]
items.text: A deep crimson, tart tea made from dried hibiscus flowers, served hot or cold and often sweetened with sugar. It's refreshing, uniquely Egyptian, and available at every café.

items.label: Sugarcane Juice [Asir Asab]
items.text: Fresh sugarcane pressed right before your eyes, often mixed with lemon or ginger for extra flavor. It's sweet, energizing, and ubiquitous at street carts; make sure the vendor uses fresh cane.

items.label: Sahlab [Warm Milk Drink]
items.text: A creamy, starchy hot milk drink flavored with vanilla, rose water, and topped with crushed pistachios and coconut. It's traditionally served in winter at dedicated sahlab stands.

items.label: Tamarind Juice [Asir Tamarind]
items.text: A tangy, deeply flavored juice made from tamarind pulp and water, sometimes sweetened. It's cooling and unique to North African cuisine.

items.label: Tap Water & Street Beverages
items.text: Avoid tap water entirely; stick to bottled water and drinks from busy, reputable vendors. Street juice stalls using tap water or questionable ice can cause stomach issues for travelers.

items.label: Local Restaurants & Grills [Masaem]
items.text: No-frills establishments with plastic chairs and grilled meat over charcoal; they're cheap, authentic, and where Egyptians actually eat. Look for crowds and minimal décor—that's a good sign.

items.label: Tourist-Oriented Nile Cruises & Rooftops
items.text: Scenic dining with sunset views, but expect inflated prices and compromised food quality; these are primarily experiences, not culinary destinations. Book through your hotel to avoid scams.

items.label: Khan el-Khalili & Bazaar Eateries
items.text: Atmospheric but often mediocre food at premium prices; order simple items like kofta and grilled meats rather than complex dishes. Arrive early to beat tour groups.

items.label: Modern Upscale Egyptian Restaurants
items.text: Places like Sequoia (Cairo) or restaurants in New Cairo offer refined takes on traditional dishes in air-conditioned comfort. They're pricier but reliable for special occasions and less adventurous eaters.

items.label: Avoiding Tourist Traps
items.text: Avoid restaurants with laminated picture menus and aggressive touts on the street; they're designed for one-time visitors. Eat where locals eat—ask your hotel staff for their favorite neighborhood spot.

items.label: Alcohol in a Muslim Country
items.text: While not prohibited, alcohol isn't widely served outside tourist areas and upscale restaurants; many Egyptians don't drink for religious reasons. Be respectful and discreet, especially during Ramadan.

items.label: Egyptian Beer [Stella & Sakara]
items.text: Local lagers that are light, crisp, and widely available in restaurants and supermarkets catering to tourists. Stella Artois is actually brewed in Egypt and tastes slightly different from the Belgian version.

items.label: Local Wine
items.text: Egyptian wines like those from Gianaclis Vineyard exist but are not widely available; imported wines are pricey in restaurants. Quality and selection are limited compared to Mediterranean destinations.

items.label: Where to Drink
items.text: High-end hotels, Nile-view restaurants, and a few upscale bars in New Cairo serve alcohol without judgment. Neighborhood bars are rare and may feel uncomfortable for foreign visitors.

guide/transport.json.title: Getting Around
guide/transport.json.subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around Egypt.

items.label: Main operators
items.text: Egyptian National Railways (ENR) operates most long-distance services. Book via the official website (enr.gov.eg) or ticket offices at major stations like Ramses Railway Station in Cairo. First and second-class sleeper cars are available on overnight routes to Aswan and Alexandria.

items.label: Key routes
items.text: Popular routes include Cairo-Alexandria (2.5 hours), Cairo-Aswan (14+ hours), and Cairo-Luxor (10 hours). Trains are slower than buses but offer more comfort and views of the Nile Valley.
Trains frequently run 1–3 hours late; plan extra time for connections. Pickpocketing occurs on crowded services, especially at Cairo Central Station—keep valuables secure.

items.label: Booking tips
items.text: Book sleeper cars 2–3 weeks ahead during peak season (October–April). Bring your passport to the ticket office; foreigners sometimes pay a different (higher) fare than Egyptians.

items.label: Cairo Metro
items.text: The Cairo Metro has three lines and is the fastest way across the city, costing 2–5 EGP per ride. Trains run 5:30 AM–midnight daily. Women-only cars are available during peak hours on most lines.
The Cairo Metro is extremely crowded during rush hours (7–9 AM and 4–7 PM)—avoid if you have heavy luggage or are uncomfortable in packed spaces. Theft and harassment are common on crowded trains.

items.label: Alexandria transport
items.text: Alexandria has a single-line tramway system that is atmospheric but slow (1.50–2 EGP). Microbuses (shared minivans) are the main transport; hail them on the street and negotiate a fare or look for a set-fare stand.

items.label: Cairo buses
items.text: Cairo's white-and-blue buses and orange microbuses crisscross the city cheaply (2–3 EGP) but routes can be confusing for visitors. Microbuses are faster but crowded; avoid night travel on any bus.
Bus strikes and service disruptions are common during peak travel seasons. Ask your hotel about current conditions before planning a trip.

items.label: White taxis
items.text: Cairo's white metered taxis are licensed and abundant. Ask your hotel to estimate a fair fare beforehand; drivers often claim meters are broken and demand inflated prices. Always agree on a fare before entering or insist on the meter.

items.label: Uber & Careem
items.text: Uber and Careem (ride-hailing apps) operate in Cairo, Alexandria, Giza, and some other cities. Fares are transparent and often cheaper than negotiating with street taxis. Both accept card payments, making them safer than cash-only taxis.

Unlicensed taxis ('black taxis') are common but risky for tourists—use only metered white taxis, ride-hailing apps, or hotel-arranged transport. Solo travelers should avoid hailing taxis late at night.

items.label: Long-distance travel
items.text: For trips outside the city (e.g., Cairo to Giza Pyramids), negotiate a round-trip fare with a taxi or use Uber. Many hotels can arrange private drivers for a full day at around 400–600 EGP.
Taxi fines for speeding and traffic violations can be steep (up to 500 EGP), but as a passenger you are not liable. Never leave valuables unattended in a taxi.

Driving in Egypt is chaotic and hazardous; road accidents are common, traffic laws are loosely enforced, and drivers often ignore signals and lane markings. Visitors unfamiliar with local driving customs should avoid renting a car and use taxis or ride-hailing instead.

items.label: Legal requirements
items.text: You need an International Driving Permit (IDP), a valid passport, and an Egyptian driving license (obtainable from rental firms). Vehicle insurance is mandatory; check that the rental includes it. The legal driving age is 18.

Police checkpoints are frequent, especially outside cities; always carry your rental agreement, license, and ID. Bribes are sometimes solicited; politely refuse and ask for an official fine receipt if cited.

items.label: Practical alternatives
items.text: Hire a driver through your hotel or a tour operator (safer and often cheaper than renting a car). For long-distance travel (Cairo to Luxor, Aswan, or the Red Sea), use intercity buses or private car services.

Speed limits are 50–80 km/h in cities and 100 km/h on highways, but enforcement is inconsistent. Speeding fines can exceed 500 EGP. Fuel stations are common in cities but sparse in remote areas; plan accordingly.

items.label: Major airports
items.text: Cairo International Airport (CAI) is the primary hub, followed by Hurghada, Sharm El-Sheikh, Aswan, and Luxor. EgyptAir is the national carrier; budget airlines include Air Arabia Maroc and FlyEgypt.

items.label: Getting to/from airports
items.text: Use Uber, Careem, or arrange a pickup through your hotel (safer and more reliable than street taxis). Some hotels offer free airport shuttle service. Cairo Airport is 20 km northeast of central Cairo; expect 45–90 minutes depending on traffic.

Arrive 3 hours before international flights and 2 hours before domestic flights; queues can be long and unpredictable. Baggage handlers at the airport may expect tips (10–20 EGP) even though service is included.

items.label: Domestic flights
items.text: Domestic flights (Cairo–Aswan, Cairo–Luxor) cost 600–1,200 EGP and save time versus trains or buses. Book via EgyptAir's website or through travel agents; prices are relatively stable year-round.
Flight delays and cancellations are common during sandstorms (spring) or high winds. Confirm your flight 24 hours in advance via the airline.

items.label: Nile cruises
items.text: Multi-day Nile cruise ships operate between Cairo, Luxor, and Aswan. Booking through travel agencies or cruise lines (e.g., Uniworld, Abercrombie & Kent) is common; all-inclusive packages range from $1,500–$5,000+ per person.

items.label: Local ferries
items.text: Ferries cross the Nile at multiple points (Cairo, Luxor, Aswan) and cost 2–5 EGP; they are scenic and efficient for reaching the West Bank (Valley of the Kings area). Service runs throughout the day.

items.label: Red Sea ferries
items.text: Ferries connect Hurghada to Sharm El-Sheikh (45 minutes) and to Aqaba, Jordan (8 hours) via operators like Sinai Star. Fares range from 150–400 EGP depending on the route and vessel class.
Ferry schedules are unpredictable; always confirm departure times and arrive early. Life jackets are often unavailable, and overcrowding is common on budget routes.

items.label: Metro & bus passes
items.text: Cairo Metro offers daily, weekly, and monthly passes (50, 140, and 250 EGP respectively) that cover unlimited trips on all three lines. Tourists often just buy single tickets (2–5 EGP) as needed.

items.label: Train & bus tickets
items.text: Book trains online via enr.gov.eg or at Ramses Station and other major hubs. Long-distance buses (Go Bus, Super Jet) can be booked at terminals or online; tickets cost 80–250 EGP depending on distance and comfort level.

Ticket touts and unofficial vendors may approach you at stations offering discounted fares; always buy from official windows or kiosks. 'Special foreigner prices' are often inflated compared to local rates.

items.label: Payment methods
items.text: Cash (Egyptian Pounds) is required for most public transport; few buses and microbuses accept cards. Metro and some bus lines are moving toward contactless cards, but check current systems when you arrive.

items.label: Local SIM cards
items.text: Egypt's main carriers—Vodafone, Etisalat, Orange, and We—sell prepaid SIM cards at kiosks, airports, and convenience stores for 5–50 EGP. Data bundles (500 MB–10 GB) range from 10–100 EGP and are valid 7–30 days.

items.label: Best for tourists
items.text: Vodafone and Etisalat have the widest networks; Orange and We offer competitive data rates. Buy a SIM at Cairo Airport for immediate connectivity (open 24/7), or wait until you reach your hotel.

Internet speeds are slow by Western standards; 4G connection is available in cities but unreliable in rural areas. The government has been known to throttle speeds and block social media sites during political unrest.

items.label: International roaming
items.text: Roaming from your home carrier is very expensive (often 5–10 EGP per MB). Buying a local SIM is far cheaper and recommended unless you only stay a few days.
Registration of SIM cards with your passport is required by law; the shopkeeper will handle this. Keep your receipt and phone number for support.
